title: Executive Summary
text: Executive Summary | The Citizen Audit Concise summary of the current published audit, its key findings, and what readers should review next. Current Published Audit Version 1.0 is presented as a bounded, evidence-first publication. Its strongest claim is not that every spending lane is fully measured, but that measurable lanes, unresolved limits, and non-additive bases are disclosed clearly enough for independent review. [{"type":"actions","links":[{"label":"Read the full audit","href":"/audit.html","variant":"primary"},{"label":"Read methodology","href":"/methodology.html"},{"label":"Browse source library","href":"/sources.html"},{"label":"Review decision log","href":"/decision-log.html"}]},{"type":"cardGrid","cards":[{"eyebrow":"Key finding","title":"The publication keeps measurable lanes on their native bases","body":"Appropriations, obligations, drawdowns, outlays, and domestic program examples are not blended into a synthetic grand total."},{"eyebrow":"Key finding","title":"The conservative total is a reproducible set of subtotals","body":"The platform preserves lane discipline and makes the supporting blueprint visible instead of asking readers to trust a flattened topline."},{"eyebrow":"Key finding","title":"Evidence gaps remain part of the publication","body":"Open questions and non-measurable programs stay visible so the site does not claim certainty where public records do not support it."},{"eyebrow":"Key finding","title":"The final argument is bounded by the same rules as the rest of the audit","body":"The publication does not introduce a looser theory at the end than it uses in the numbered sections."}]},{"type":"panel","heading":"Major takeaways","contentBlocks":[{"type":"list","items":["This is a verification-oriented audit, not a persuasion-first narrative.","Some lanes are publishable now; others remain governed by missing federal breakout data.","Domestic examples such as ORR, emergency Medicaid, and SSI are treated differently because the public record supports them differently.","The gap register is itself a substantive transparency finding."]}]},{"type":"panel","heading":"What to review next","stack":true,"texts":["If you are testing the work, read methodology before comparing numbers across sections. title: Methodology
text: Methodology | The Citizen Audit Methodology rules, evidence classes, and basis-discipline for The Citizen Audit. Rules Before Results The site preserves the audit's core discipline: measure only what the public record supports, label every number by type and basis, and document gaps rather than manufacturing certainty. [{"type":"actions","links":[{"label":"How to verify this work","href":"/verify.html","variant":"primary"},{"label":"Browse source library","href":"/sources.html"},{"label":"Review decision log","href":"/decision-log.html"},{"label":"Read the full audit","href":"/audit.html"}]},{"type":"panel","heading":"Binding Rules","table":{"headers":["Rule","Application"],"rows":[["Gross, not net","Figures measure money directed toward non-citizens or non-U.S. recipients, not net fiscal impact."],["Total A / Total B","Total A is gross federal resources. title: How To Verify This Work
text:
How to Verify This Work | The Citizen Audit Step-by-step verification guidance for readers reviewing The Citizen Audit. Verification Path Readers should not trust the audit blindly. The publication is designed so claims can be checked against methodology rules, source records, archived artifacts where available, and documented decision history. [{"type":"actions","links":[{"label":"Read methodology","href":"/methodology.html","variant":"primary"},{"label":"Browse source library","href":"/sources.html"},{"label":"Open decision log","href":"/decision-log.html"},{"label":"Challenge the audit","href":"/challenge.html"}]},{"type":"panel","heading":"Verification checklist","contentBlocks":[{"type":"list","ordered":true,"items":["Read the methodology so basis discipline, exclusions, and evidence classes are clear before comparing numbers.","Open the source pages attached to the claim or section you want to test.","Review archive links or alternate official artifacts where available.","Check the decision log entries that explain inclusion, exclusion, or reconciliation choices.","Compare the published claim against the primary source record whenever the source library points to one.","If you believe a claim fails the record, submit a challenge with evidence rather than a generalized objection."]}]},{"type":"cardGrid","cards":[{"eyebrow":"Method first","title":"Do not compare unlike figures","body":"A disagreement is not resolved until the figure type, accounting stage, and beneficiary basis match."},{"eyebrow":"Source trail","title":"Start with primary records where possible","body":"The source library labels official and secondary artifacts so readers can prioritize the strongest available evidence."},{"eyebrow":"Transparency rule","title":"Treat open questions as part of the result","body":"If a lane still depends on missing records, that limit belongs in the evaluation rather than outside it."}]},{"type":"panel","heading":"What a serious challenge should do","stack":true,"texts":["Identify the exact claim, page, or section involved. title: CRS IF10183
text: Corroborating conduit for the Section 3 FY2023 foreign-assistance obligations total of approximately $99.9B. Section 1 Section 3 Corroborates the published FY2023 foreign-assistance obligations figure used in Section 3. Supports the audit's separation between obligations and disbursements.  Congressional Research Service via Congress.gov Issue brief Secondary https://www.congress.gov/crs-product/IF10183 verified Verified against current Congress.gov and CRS citation metadata for CRS product IF10183. Congress.gov blocked direct automated fetches during this pass, so the canonical product page and official CRS PDF artifact were confirmed from current index metadata and linked citations.

title: 8 U.S.C. Sec. 1641(b)
text: Qualified-alien definition used throughout the domestic eligibility framework. Section 2 Section 8 Section 9 Defines the lawful-status categories used in domestic-program analysis. Supports the rule against collapsing different immigration-status categories into one figure.  Office of the Law Revision Counsel, U.S. House of Representatives Statute Primary https://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml?req=%28title%3A8+section%3A1641+edition%3Aprelim%29 verified Official U.S. Code page verified. No separate archive snapshot was verified during this pass.

title: 8 U.S.C. Sec. 1611
text: Federal public-benefit restriction baseline for non-qualified aliens, including the emergency-medical exception structure. Section 2 Section 7 Section 8 Section 9 Supports the default ineligibility baseline for undocumented immigrants in most federal benefits. Provides the emergency-medical exception framework carried into Section 7. A-037 Office of the Law Revision Counsel, U.S. House of Representatives Statute Primary https://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml?edition=prelim&num=0&req=granuleid%3AUSC-prelim-title8-section1611 verified Official U.S. Code page verified. No separate archive snapshot was verified during this pass.

title: 8 U.S.C. Sec. 1613
text: Five-year bar and related exceptions for federal means-tested public benefits. Section 2 Section 8 Section 9 Supports the methodology distinction between qualified categories and waiting-period restrictions.  Office of the Law Revision Counsel, U.S. House of Representatives Statute Primary https://uscode.house.gov/quicksearch/get.plx?section=1613&title=8 verified Official U.S. Code page verified. No separate archive snapshot was verified during this pass.

title: GAO Budget Glossary (GAO-05-734SP)
text: Primary budget-accounting terminology for appropriation, obligation, outlay, and number-type separation. Section 2 Section 3 Defines appropriation, obligation, and budget authority for the audit's accounting rules. Supports the rule that unlike number types cannot be merged.  U.S. Government Accountability Office Glossary Primary https://www.gao.gov/products/gao-05-734sp verified Official GAO product page verified, with the official PDF retained as the alternate stable artifact.

title: CBO Common Budgetary Terms Explained
text: Companion budgetary definitions used to corroborate number-type discipline throughout the publication. Section 2 Reinforces the audit's separation of appropriations, obligations, outlays, and projections.  Congressional Budget Office Glossary Primary https://www.cbo.gov/publication/57660 verified Canonical CBO landing page verified. The official PDF download provides alternate stable access to the same publication.

title: ForeignAssistance.gov financial datasets
text: Primary aggregate dataset for foreign-assistance obligations and disbursements used in Section 3. Section 1 Section 3 Section 6 Supports the FY2023 obligations figure of approximately $99.9B. Supports the FY2023 disbursements figure of approximately $71.9B. Supports the FY2024 obligations figure of approximately $85.8B and later reported-to-date figures. A-005 U.S. Department of State / USAID / reporting agencies Dataset landing page Primary https://foreignassistance.gov/data verified Canonical data-portal URL verified. No separate archived snapshot or static export URL was verified during this pass.

title: Pew foreign-aid reporting summary
text: Corroborating conduit for FY2023 foreign-assistance disbursements, agency composition, and military-share context. Section 3 Corroborates the FY2023 disbursement total near $71.9B. Corroborates agency and military-share breakdowns used in Section 3. A-005 Pew Research Center Research summary Secondary https://www.pewresearch.org/short-reads/2025/02/06/what-the-data-says-about-us-foreign-aid/ verified Verified to the current Pew foreign-aid explainer that cites ForeignAssistance.gov and the FY2023 disbursement frame used as corroboration in Section 3. No alternate official archive artifact was identified during this pass.

title: USAFacts foreign-aid reporting summary
text: Corroborating conduit for later-year obligations in the international-assistance lane. Section 3 Corroborates approximately $85.8B FY2024 obligations and the lower-confidence FY2025 to-date and FY2026 to-date figures. A-005 USAFacts Data answer page Secondary https://usafacts.org/answers/how-much-foreign-aid-does-the-us-provide/country/united-states/ verified Verified to the current USAFacts foreign-aid answer page that reports the FY2024, FY2025, and FY2026 obligation figures used as corroboration in Section 3. No official archived artifact was identified during this pass.

title: State Supplementary Tables FY2025
text: Account-level foreign-assistance tables retained for next-pass pulls and account composition checks. Section 3 Supports account-level breakdowns behind the international-assistance universe.  U.S. Department of State Budget tables PDF Primary https://www.state.gov/wp-content/uploads/2024/03/Supplementary-Tables-Foreign-Assistance.pdf verified Verified against the State Department PDF path.

title: CRS IF11437
text: Used to distinguish taxpayer-funded military aid from allied-funded foreign military sales. Section 3 Section 4 Section 5 Supports exclusion of allied-funded FMS from taxpayer totals. Supports the Section 3 to Section 5 netting rule around military-aid overlap. A-017 Congressional Research Service via Congress.gov Issue brief Secondary https://www.congress.gov/crs-product/IF11437 verified Congress.gov blocked automated retrieval during this pass, so verification relied on current web-discoverable official CRS product and PDF URL patterns rather than a live fetch of the final page body.

title: UkraineOversight.gov
text: Primary oversight source for Ukraine appropriated-versus-disbursed context used in the illustrative Section 4 lane. Section 4 Supports the publication's Ukraine stage and delivery-context framing without making Section 4 additive to the totals.  UkraineOversight.gov Oversight portal Primary https://www.ukraineoversight.gov/Funding/ verified Verified against the official oversight portal.

title: State Department Ukraine/Israel aid reporting
text: Primary State reporting used in the illustrative Section 4 examples for identifiable aid flows. Section 4 Section 5 Supports identifiable Ukraine and Israel aid lines discussed in Section 4.  U.S. Department of State Agency reporting Primary https://www.state.gov/bureau-of-political-military-affairs/releases/2025/01/u-s-security-cooperation-with-ukraine verified Verified to the State Department's January 9, 2025 Ukraine security-cooperation fact sheet, with the archived 2021-2025 State site captured as the alternate official artifact. Israel FMF and missile-defense appropriation context remains separately sourced through S-049.

title: GAO drawdown valuation reporting
text: Used for the reported drawdown overvaluation issue and to keep transfer value separate from replacement appropriations. Section 4 Section 5 Supports the GAO-reported drawdown misvaluation issue. Supports excluding drawdown transfer value from net-new taxpayer replacement totals.  U.S. Government Accountability Office Oversight report Primary https://www.gao.gov/products/gao-24-106934 verified Verified against the GAO product page.

title: New analysis from Economists for Ukraine: The cost of U.S. aid to Ukraine is less than half the official figures
text: Retained as disconfirming or bounding context for delivered-value discussions in Section 4. Section 4 Provides lower-bound contextual estimates for value reaching Ukraine without being adopted as the canonical total.  Centre for Economic Policy Research Contextual analysis Secondary https://cepr.org/voxeu/columns/new-analysis-economists-ukraine-cost-us-aid-ukraine-less-half-official-figures verified Normalized from a generic CEPR placeholder to the specific CEPR VoxEU article that matches the audit's contrary-context use. The Economists for Ukraine analysis is retained as the alternate stable supporting artifact.

title: Council on Foreign Relations aid tracker context
text: Used as contextual corroboration for Section 4's illustrative examples. Section 4 Corroborates broad Ukraine and Israel aid-stage context without serving as the primary figure source. text: Presidential Drawdown Authority values can diverge from replacement cost, and valuation ambiguity can create false inflation if the wrong measure is reused elsewhere. The publication treats drawdown value and replacement appropriation separately and carries the valuation problem as a reporting-quality issue. The publication keeps Total B open for these military cases rather than inventing a capture rate. A primary record separating transferred WRSA value from stock that remains U.S.-owned or merely positioned. Section 5 S-050

A lawful harmonization method that keeps measure type, period, and duplication risks separated. Section 5 S-043

text: Tax-credit flows can involve ITIN filers and citizen children, which makes noncitizen-specific attribution impossible without a published IRS breakout. Section 9 names the gap and routes it to Section 13 instead of deriving a figure from mixed-status tax filing patterns. text: Grant-funded housing programs that do not verify immigration status cannot easily produce a status-based spending breakout. A published program-level record showing whether any status breakout exists inside no-verification housing grants. Section 10 S-067 S-068

title: Federal K-12 noncitizen spending
text: K-12 schooling is constitutionally status-blind, so the public record may not be able to produce a status-based federal spending line at all. Section 11 treats this as a legal collection limit rather than a simple missing dataset. No ordinary status-based spending dataset is expected; any lawful resolution would need to respect Plyler's status-collection limits. Section 11 S-069

title: Eligible-noncitizen federal student aid
text: Undocumented students are barred from federal aid, but eligible-noncitizen aid is still not published as a separate spending line. Section 11 names the eligibility boundary clearly and routes the missing eligible-noncitizen spending breakout to Section 13. A Department of Education breakout isolating federal student-aid amounts for eligible noncitizens. Section 11 S-069

title: CCDF, SSBG, CSBG, and LIHEAP no-breakout
text: State-administered federal streams can lose citizenship detail in the federal-state-local chain, preventing a clean noncitizen spending breakout. Section 12 documents the reconciliation role and routes these no-breakout programs into the Section 13 gap register. Published program breakouts showing whether any of these state-administered federal streams isolate status-level spending. Section 12 Section 13

title: Per-program responsible agency for missing status data
text: Readers need to know which agency would have to publish a missing status-based breakout before a gap can ever be resolved. Section 13 frames this as part of the gap register rather than pretending every missing line has the same administrative owner. A program-by-program ownership map tying each missing breakout to the responsible federal reporting agency. Section 13

title: Whether Class C or D modeled estimates exist as bounded context only
text: Modeled or secondary estimates may exist for some gaps, but using them as adopted totals would violate the audit's evidence hierarchy unless they are clearly bounded as context only. Section 13 leaves this open instead of backfilling opaque programs with secondary modeled estimates. A bounded inventory of Class C or D estimates that can be published as context without replacing missing primary evidence. Section 13 S-076

title: Federal-only emergency Medicaid share
text: The site can publish the federal-plus-state emergency Medicaid figure used in the locked section, but not the federal-only share needed for a cleaner federal total. The limitation is carried forward explicitly in the Executive Summary and Section 7. A public federal-only emergency Medicaid spending breakout for the same period and population basis. Section 1 Section 7 S-003 S-064 S-065 S-078 S-079

title: Gross, not net
text: Every adopted figure is gross money directed toward non-U.S. recipients or noncitizens. The audit does not offset those figures against taxes paid or any net-fiscal-impact model. Section 1 Section 2 Section 9

title: Status categories are never collapsed
text: Lawful permanent residents, refugees, asylees, parolees, temporary statuses, and undocumented persons are analytically distinct categories and are never merged into one undifferentiated headline. Section 2

title: Number type and resource category are recorded separately
text: Each figure keeps both its number type and its resource category visible. Drawdown transfer value, replacement appropriations, obligations, outlays, and program authorities are tracked as different things. Section 2 Section 5

title: Universe-of-programs completeness artifact
text: Sections with broad program scope publish their universe explicitly, including residual or immaterial lines, so no material account is silently dropped from the research frame. Section 3 Section 4 Section 6

title: The Two Running Totals
